name: Grammar: Tenses (Simple, Continuous, Perfect, and Conditional Sentences)

sourceRefs.label: 2022_q page 25 Q93, page 12 Q43 (real, with answer key marks)
2025_q page 130 Q144, page 139 Q173 & Q175 (real, with answer key marks)
2024_q page 89 Q187 & Q188 (real, with answer key marks)

researchNotes: Third Grammar topic. Unusually dense real evidence — 7 confirmed hits across 3 different exam years (2022, 2024, 2025), all independently verified against scanned answer-key checkmarks. Real exam tense questions cluster around 2 patterns: (1) picking the correct future-tense variant (simple/continuous/perfect) for a given time clue ('tomorrow', 'by the time', 'in two years'), and (2) conditional-sentence tense agreement (if-clause tense must match the correct main-clause tense). Bonus non-tense items from the same source pages (passive/active voice, gerund-after-verb, question tags) stashed for their own upcoming Unit I topics. Expansion pass (Batch 16): added a time-clue-to-tense matching trick and the second conditional (distinguishing it from first/third).

reading.intro: Real TNPSC tense questions mostly test future-tense variants (matched to time clues) and conditional-sentence tense agreement — not abstract tense theory.

qa.question: Real exam: "The Doctor ___ the operation tomorrow." Which tense fits?
qa.answer: Will perform — simple future, matching the explicit future time clue 'tomorrow'.

qa.question: Real exam: "In two years' time I ___ (earn) my degree." Which tense fits?
qa.answer: Shall have earned — future perfect tense, used because the action (earning the degree) will be COMPLETED by a specific future point in time ('in two years' time').

qa.question: Real exam: "Will you be doing laundry tonight?" What tense form is this question itself in?
qa.answer: Future continuous tense ('will be doing') — describes an action expected to be in progress at a specific future time.

qa.question: Real exam: "She ___ an exam at 2 p.m. tomorrow." Which tense fits, and why not simple future?
qa.answer: Will be taking — future continuous, because the exam is an ongoing action happening AT a specific future time (2 p.m. tomorrow), not simply a one-off future event ('will take' would suggest a single, instantaneous action rather than an exam in progress at that time).

qa.question: Real exam: "The plane ___ by the time I got to the airport." Which tense fits?
qa.answer: Had left — past perfect tense, since the plane's departure happened BEFORE another past action (arriving at the airport) — the classic 'earlier of two past events' use of past perfect.

qa.question: Real exam (first conditional): "If she works hard, she ___ a scholarship." Which tense fits in the main clause?
qa.answer: Will get — first conditional structure: 'If' + simple present (works) → simple future (will get), used for a realistic/likely future outcome.

qa.question: Real exam (third conditional): "If you ___ hard you would have won the relay match." Which tense fits in the if-clause?
qa.answer: Had worked — third conditional structure: 'If' + past perfect (had worked) → 'would have' + past participle (would have won), used for an unreal/hypothetical PAST situation (the relay match is already over and was NOT won).

qa.question: What is a quick trick for matching time-clue words to the correct future-tense variant?
qa.answer: 'Tomorrow/next week' + single event → SIMPLE FUTURE ('will do'). 'At [specific time]' + an action in progress → FUTURE CONTINUOUS ('will be doing'). 'By [a future point]' + a completed action → FUTURE PERFECT ('will/shall have done'). Spot the time-clue phrase FIRST, then pick the matching tense pattern.
qa.tip: 'By the time'/'by [date]' almost always signals a PERFECT tense (future perfect or past perfect) — the action must be complete relative to that reference point, not just simple past/future.

qa.question: What is the second conditional structure, and how does it differ from the first conditional?
qa.answer: SECOND CONDITIONAL: 'If' + simple past → 'would' + base verb — used for an UNREAL/hypothetical PRESENT or future situation (e.g., 'If I had wings, I would fly'). This differs from the FIRST conditional ('If' + simple present → 'will' + base verb), which describes a REALISTIC/likely future outcome, not a hypothetical one.

questions.prompt: "In two years' time I ___ (earn) my degree."
"Will you be doing laundry tonight?" is in which tense?
"The plane ___ by the time I got to the airport."
"If she works hard, she ___ a scholarship."
"If you ___ hard you would have won the relay match."
"If I ___ wings, I would fly." Which conditional/tense fits?
questions.trick: Second conditional = simple past in the if-clause + 'would' in the main clause, for something unreal/impossible right now (I don't have wings).

questions.prompt: Which time-clue phrase most strongly signals a PERFECT tense is needed?
questions.explanation: 'By the time' / 'by [date]' signals a perfect tense.
